votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the logic of the Coq specification, including the length check, modulo arithmetic, and the string splitting/concatenation required for the circular shift operation.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, accurately translating the string slicing and reversal logic for the circular shift.
The Python code correctly implements the logic of the Coq specification, including the conditional check for shift size, the modulo operation for circular shifting, and the string reversal case.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec: when len(s) < shift, it returns the reversed string; otherwise it computes k = shift % len(s), and if k == 0 returns s unchanged, else splits s into u (first len(s)-k chars) and v (last k chars) and returns v + u. This matches the existential condition in Coq where s = u ++ v with len(v) = k and res = v ++ u.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec: when len(s) < shift, it returns the reversed string; otherwise it computes k = shift mod len(s), and if k=0 returns s unchanged, else splits s into u (first len(s)-k chars) and v (last k chars) and checks if res = v + u. The existential in Coq is satisfied by exactly this split where v has length k and u is the prefix, which Python computes directly.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec: when len(s) < shift, it returns the reversed string; otherwise it computes k = shift mod len(s), and if k=0 returns s unchanged, else splits s into u (first len(s)-k chars) and v (last k chars) and returns v+u. The existential in Coq is satisfied by the specific u,v chosen in Python (s[:-k] and s[-k:]), which have the required properties: s = u ++ v, len(v) = k, and res = v ++ u.

Matches the spec: if shift > len(s) return reverse(s); else let k = shift % len(s), return s if k=0, otherwise rotate by taking last k chars to the front. The chosen u,v satisfy the existential uniquely. Assumes nonnegative shift and the same str_of_Z/reverse_string.

The Python code mirrors the Coq spec: it computes s from x, returns reverse(s) if shift > len(s), else sets k = shift mod len(s), returns s if k=0, otherwise rotates by taking the last k chars to the front. This matches the existential decomposition in Coq for all concrete inputs (with nonnegative shift).

Matches the spec: if shift > len(s) return reverse(s); else k = shift mod len(s), if k=0 return s, otherwise rotate last k chars to front. The existential in Coq corresponds to the unique suffix/prefix split used in Python. Assuming shift is nonnegative and consistent str/reverse functions, results agree on all inputs.
